summary: 11 concrete items surfaced from 22 qualifying front-page stories.

items.name: OpenKnowledge
items.source_story_title: Show HN: OpenKnowledge – open source AI-first alternative to Obsidian/Notion
items.summary: Local-first WYSIWYG markdown editor with Claude, Codex, and Cursor integrations.
items.why_included: Commenters wanted a Git-synced team knowledgebase with native AI integration, and this fits that gap.
items.evidence: One commenter described exactly that team-sync workflow need; others asked for openai-compatible providers and praised the app’s AI-first approach.

items.name: Ory
items.summary: Open-source and commercial identity stack, including Hydra, Kratos, and Ladon.
items.why_included: The thread surfaced explicit praise for Ory as a practical auth platform.
items.evidence: An Ory Hydra author pointed readers to github.com/ory and ory.com, while other commenters called Ory services a delight and singled out Hydra, Kratos, and Ladon.

items.name: GPU back end for Emacs
items.source_story_title: I built a GPU back end for Emacs
items.summary: Emacs backend that pushes rendering work onto the GPU for sharper UI effects.
items.why_included: Commenters wanted better cursor and highlight effects in Emacs, so the backend feels immediately useful.
items.evidence: The thread compared it with Ghostty’s cursor_blaze effect and Emacs packages like pulse.el and beacon, arguing for more powerful visual feedback.

items.name: OS9Map
items.summary: Lightweight map app for classic Mac OS 9 machines with tiny RAM requirements.
items.why_included: Vintage Mac users immediately wanted to run it on real hardware.
items.evidence: Commenters praised the 16 MB required / 32 MB recommended footprint and said they would try it on PowerBook Pismo and other OS 9 battlestations.

items.name: Chess-Inspired Roguelike
items.summary: Deterministic chess-like roguelike with seeded runs and solver-friendly movement.
items.why_included: HN reaction was strongly positive and unusually analytical for a game thread.
items.evidence: Commenters called it fun, intuitive, pretty, and addictive, and several noted that its seeded RNG and deterministic movement make it solver-friendly.

items.name: Pieces of the Kingdom
items.summary: Chess-like roguelike demo on Steam with weird puzzle energy.
items.why_included: It was directly recommended in the thread by a creator building a similar game.
items.evidence: A commenter said they were working on a different chess-like roguelike and told people to check it out if they like weird chess puzzles, bad chess puns, and Hnefatafl.

items.name: Half-Life 2 in a Browser
items.summary: Browser-playable Half-Life 2 port that ran straight into classic-game comparisons.
items.why_included: The thread quickly turned into a list of other playable browser classics.
items.evidence: Commenters immediately added Quake 3, Unreal Tournament, RuneScape 2, Ultima Online, and similar browser-playable projects as references.

items.name: noclip.website
items.summary: Browser explorer for classic game levels and maps.
items.why_included: It was singled out as a richer companion for exploring old game spaces.
items.evidence: One commenter called it a place with hundreds of levels from dozens of older games, including Half-Life 2, and suggested it as a better exploration tool than the port itself.

items.name: LingoChunk
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Turn native language audio into flashcards and shadowing practice
items.summary: Audio-to-flashcards language app for shadowing, grammar help, and multilingual practice.
items.why_included: The author showed a clear real-world workflow and commenters wanted to try it.
items.evidence: The post described German and Greek study workflows, AI prompts for grammar and nuance, and asked for public-domain audio sources for the try page.

items.name: ScrollPrize
items.source_story_title: An entire Herculaneum scroll has been read for the first time
items.summary: Scroll-reading project and paper tied to the first readable Herculaneum scroll.
items.why_included: This was a real technical milestone, not just a headline.
items.evidence: The discussion praised the segmentation, unwrapping, and ink-detection work, and commenters repeatedly emphasized how huge the achievement was and how open the data was.

items.name: Oxide computer 3D rack guided tour
items.summary: Interactive 3D tour of Oxide’s rack hardware and server design.
items.why_included: Engineers responded like they wanted to work with the hardware, which is strong signal.
items.evidence: Commenters called the product a breath of fresh air, praised the integrated hardware/software design, and recommended Oxide’s podcast and original “On The Metal” show.
